coredata.dc:description: Utilizing photocatalysis to degrade antibiotics in wastewater is a vital strategy. Here, a Z-scheme heterojunction was synthesized, consisting of defect-rich porous g-C3N4 and MoO3, which exhibits remarkable photocatalytic degradation of tetracycline (TC). The results revealed that the optimal heterojunction sample exhibited a 2.47-fold enhancement of degradation efficiency compared with porous g-C3N4-x. Characterization analysis shows that the significant enhancement is attributed to the synergistic effects arising from the Z-scheme heterostructure and the introduction of nitrogen (N) defects, which collectively enhance the charge separation and light absorption capabilities of the material. Electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) spectroscopy and free radical quenching experiments indicate that the vital degradation mechanism is the assault of •O2 – and •OH on tetracycline. Additionally, the LC-MS studies demonstrated possible intermediates and degradation pathway of TC. The toxicity simulation analysis revealed that the heterojunction sample effectively mitigated the toxicity of tetracycline solution. This catalyst exhibits promising potential for applications in the elimination of antibiotics from aquatic environments.

head.ce:title: Constructing g-C3N4-x/MoO3 Z-scheme heterojunction for photodegradation of tetracycline

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Faculty of Science, Kunming University of Science and Technology, Kunming 650093, China

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Physics, Faculty of Science, Kunming University of Science and Technology, Kunming 650093, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Utilizing photocatalysis to degrade antibiotics in wastewater is a vital strategy. Here, a Z-scheme heterojunction was synthesized, consisting of defect-rich porous g-C3N4 and MoO3, which exhibits remarkable photocatalytic degradation of tetracycline (TC). The results revealed that the optimal heterojunction sample exhibited a 2.47-fold enhancement of degradation efficiency compared with porous g-C3N4-x. Characterization analysis shows that the significant enhancement is attributed to the synergistic effects arising from the Z-scheme heterostructure and the introduction of nitrogen (N) defects, which collectively enhance the charge separation and light absorption capabilities of the material. Electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) spectroscopy and free radical quenching experiments indicate that the vital degradation mechanism is the assault of •O2 – and •OH on tetracycline. Additionally, the LC-MS studies demonstrated possible intermediates and degradation pathway of TC. The toxicity simulation analysis revealed that the heterojunction sample effectively mitigated the toxicity of tetracycline solution. This catalyst exhibits promising potential for applications in the elimination of antibiotics from aquatic environments.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, the overuse of antibiotics, particularly tetracycline, has caused great harm to the water environment due to its difficulty to in removing in the natural environment [1–3]. Recognizing the potential risks, the European Medicines Agency (EMA) has established a threshold concentration of 10 ng/L for antibiotics in aquatic systems [4]. Bacteria exposed to high levels of tetracycline for a long time will develop resistance, form resistance genes and spread in the environment, with ecological toxicity [5]. Hence, there is a compelling necessity to devise an efficient and green approach for the removal of residual tetracycline. Semiconductor photocatalytic technique is considered as one of the most valid techniques for tetracycline degradation, due to its high degradation efficiency, non-toxic and environmental protection [6–8]. Traditional semiconductor materials, such as TiO2, and ZnO, exhibit low catalytic efficiency due to their inefficient solar energy utilization. Therefore, designing and fabricating an efficient tetracycline degradation photocatalyst is essential not only for protecting ecological environment but also for ensuring the sustainable use of water resources.

Graphitized carbon nitride (g-C3N4) is a 2D graphene-like semiconductor material that exhibits exceptional thermal properties, distinct electronic properties, and chemical stability compared to traditional semiconductor [9].Therefore, it is extensively used in the research of contaminant degradation in a water eco-environment under light conditions [10,11]. However, the photocatalytic performance of a single g-C3N4 material may indeed be less than ideal, which is usually due to its limited light absorption range, easy recombination of photogenerated electron-hole pairs, and insufficient catalytic active sites. In order to improve the photocatalytic performance of a single g-C3N4 material, various strategies have been usually adopted, such as element doping [12], heterojunction construction [13,14], morphology regulation [15] and surface modification [16,17].

The construction of heterostructures has emerged as a promising strategy to significantly enhance the separation of photogenerated charges in g-C3N4 [18–20]. Numerous composites of g-C3N4 with other semiconductors such as Bi2WO6 [21], FeWO4 [22], SnO2 [23], TiO2 [24] etc, have been reported to form heterojunction, which is beneficial for improving photocatalytic efficiency. Among these metal oxides, molybdenum trioxide (MoO3) has garnered significant attention due to its two-dimensional layered structure, abundant chemical valence, and strong adsorption capacity for degradation [25,26]. MoO3, with its higher valence band potential, is a prime candidate for Z-scheme heterostructure, due to its band structure matching with g-C3N4. The Z-scheme heterostructure created when MoO3 and g-C3N4 integration can compensate for the poor oxidation capacity of g-C3N4, further enhancing its overall catalytic efficiency. Several studies have already confirmed that the construction of the MoO3/g-C3N4 heterojunction effectively enhances photocatalytic performance. Hu et al. [27] designed and synthesized MoO3/g-C3N4 heterostructure catalyst by secondary thermal condensation, which achieving 100 % sulfur removal rate within 100 min. Similarly, Chen et al. [28] successfully synthesized B-doped g-C3N4 combined with MoO3, achieving complete degradation of RhB within 24 min. However, past studies on the MoO3/g-C3N4 heterojunction have notably lacked attention to the self-defect control of g-C3N4 and have not included a toxicity analysis of tetracycline. The self-defects introduce defect levels within the bandgaps of g-C3N4, effectively expanding its absorption spectrum to encompass a wider range of the visible light region [29,30]. Therefore, it remains of utmost significance to further investigate the influence of nitrogen defects on the performance of the Z-type heterojunction and conduct a thorough analysis of the toxicity of tetracycline following degradation.

Herein, molybdenum trioxide was incorporated into the nitrogen defect-rich g-C3N4 to prepare g-C3N4-x/MoO3 Z-scheme heterojunction using an ordinary blending calcination approach. The degradation activity of tetracycline by the g-C3N4-x/MoO3 heterojunction was evaluated under simulated visible light. The intermediate products of TC were analyzed by LC-MS. The Toxicity Estimation Software Tool (T.E.S.T.) was utilized to assess the toxicities of the degradation products, aiming to provide further understanding of the degradation process in practical applications. The optical properties and charge transfer ability of the synthesized g-C3N4-x/MoO3 heterojunction photocatalyst have been thoroughly investigated using multiple characterization techniques. The potential mechanism for enhancing photocatalytic performance was proposed through active substance capture and ESR analysis.

Ammonium molybdate ((NH4)2MoO4), ethanol (C2H5OH), tetracycline (C22H24N2O8), Melamine, P-benzoquinone (PBQ), iso-propanol (IPA), triethanolamine (TEOA), 2-Methoxyethanol (C3H8O2), citric acid (C6H8O7) were of the grade of analytical reagents (AR) and employed without additional purification. All solutions were prepared using ultrapure water with a resistivity of 18.25 MΩ·cm.

MoO3 was obtained via a sol–gel precursor combined with an annealing route. Specifically, 0.5 g (NH4)2MoO4 and 1.5 g citric acid were dissolved in 2-methoxyethano to generate a sol–gel precursor. Subsequently, the prepared precursor solution was sintered at 500 °C in a preheated oven for 12 h to obtain MoO3 samples.
Detailed information regarding the specific preparation methods for nitrogen defect-rich porous g-C3N4-x and bulk g-C3N4 were provided in the accompanying Supporting Information.

g-C3N4-x/MoO3 heterojunction photocatalyst (CNM) were obtained via an ordinary blending calcination approach. Firstly, 0.95 g of g-C3N4-x and 0.05 g of the prepared MoO3 were mixed in 50 ml of ethanol and subjected to ultrasonic agitation for 3 h, then the mixture was dried at 60 °C for 12 h. The final process involved the thorough milling of the powder blend for a duration of 0.5 h, followed by a sintering process at 300 °C for 4 h. After cooling, the products were gathered and given the designation CNM-5. According to the identical synthesis route (show in Scheme 1 ), CNM heterojunction with different MoO3 mass ratios of 1 wt%, 3 wt%, 5 wt%, 7 wt%, and 10 wt% were manufactured. These photocatalysts were subsequently labeled as CNM-1, CNM-3, CNM-5, CNM-7 and CNM-10, respectively.

The crystal architecture of the catalysts was examined using X-ray diffractometer (XRD, Rigaku Ultma IV., Japan). The scanning speed of the XRD is 5°/min, with a working voltage of 50 kV and a working current of 40 mA. The scanning range is from 5° to 90°. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM, ZEISS Gemini 300., Germany) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM, FEI talos F200x G2., USA) were utilized to describe the microstructure morphologies and elemental composition of the CNM. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopic (FTIR, Niolet iN10., USA) and X-ray photo-electron spectroscopic (XPS, Thermo Scientific K-Alpha., USA) were utilized to collect further information on the materials structure. The electron paramagnetic resonance spectroscopy (EPR, Bruker EMXplus-6/1., Germany) was employed to explore the existence of N defects in the systems under light conditions. The absorption performance and the band gaps were estimated through an UV−vis diffuse reflectance spectrum (UV−vis DRS, U-4100., Hitachi, Japan) with BaSO4 materials serving as the baseline. Fluorescence spectrophotometry (FS, F-7000., Japan) was utilized to detect the photoluminescence (PL) of materials at a wavelength excitation of 385 nm. Electron spin resonance spectroscopy (ESR, Bruker EMXplus-9.5/12., Germany) was used to identify the formation of radicals during the photocatalytic processes.

The photocatalytic ability of the sample was investigated through tetracycline photodegradation using a simulated visible light source: a 300 W Xenon lamp equipped with a filter ( λ ≥ 400 nm). The experiment was conducted under conditions of a neutral pH of 7, at room temperature, and in an environment consistent with the ambient water conditions. Specifically, 50 mg of the catalyst were distributed into 100 ml of 20 mg/L TC solution. The suspension solution was agitated in a dark condition for 30 min to achieve the equilibrium of absorption and desorption. Under continuous irradiation, 3 ml sample was sampled from the solution at intervals of 10 min, then processed the solid catalyst with syringe filters (0.22 μm). The content of TC in the filtrate was evaluated by analyzing variations of the prominent absorption peaks (357 nm) in the ultraviolet − visible range of TC using a spectrophotometer (DR6000，USA). The photodegradation efficiency (η, %) of TC was computed utilizing the following formula:
(1)

C 0

× 100 %

Where

represents initial concentration of TC solution, while

represents the filtrate concentration when the illumination time is t minute.
Meanwhile, to investigate the principal reactive species, PBQ, IPA and TEOA were employed as scavengers for superoxide radical (•O2 –), hydroxyl radical (•OH) and hole (h+), respectively.

Photocurrent density measurements were recorded using the CHI660 (Shanghai, China) electrochemical instrument fitted with a conventional three-electrode electrochemical setup. The prepared catalysts were used as working electrode on ITO, platinum (Pt) sheet and the saturated (Ag/AgCl) silver chloride were employed as opposite electrode and reference electrode, respectively. For preparing the working electrode, 5 mg of as-obtained catalysts were well dispersed in 5 ml of N, N-Dimethylformamide (DMF). 0.5 M sodium sulfate solution (Na2SO4) used as electrolyte for recording Photocurrent.

The toxicities of the intermediates generated during the photo-degradation were assessed via the quantitative structure–activity relationship (QSAR) method-based Toxicity Estimation Software Tool (T.E.S.T.).

The microstructure of the prepared catalysts was explored using SEM and TEM. The g-C3N4-x displayed a porous sheet-like structure (Fig. S1b) with a larger specific surface area compared to the bulk structure g-C3N4 (Fig. S1a), which is favorable for the formation of heterojunctions. After introducing MoO3 into porous g-C3N4-x (Fig. 1 (a-b)), it could be revealed that the MoO3 particles were closely adhered to the surface of g-C3N4-x. Further analysis using HRTEM (Fig. 1c) revealed lattice spacings 0.324 nm aligned with the (021) orthorhombic crystal plane of MoO3. The lattice structure of g-C3N4-x remains indistinct, which may be due to its amorphous properties or the more blurred lattice fringes. A distinct heterojunction interface between the two semiconductor materials was evident. Furthermore, the elemental mapping revealed that the C, N, O, and Mo elements were uniformly distributed throughout CNM-5, and the signals from g-C3N4-x and MoO3 overlapped, as depicted in Fig. 1(d-g). This observation indicates that the CNM heterojunction has been successfully synthesized.

The powder XRD patterns of the materials were revealed in Fig. 2 a. The XRD peaks of g-C3N4-x samples were well matched to these of pure g-C3N4 (JCPDS 87-1526), with two distinct peaks at 27.6° and 13.8° that corresponds to the diffraction planes (002) and (100), respectively [31,32]. The XRD pattern of MoO3 closely matched orthorhombic molybdenum oxide (JCPDS 05-0508) [33]. When MoO3 was coupled with g-C3N4-x, mixed diffraction peaks of MoO3 and g-C3N4-x appeared in the CNM heterojunctions, without any new diffraction peaks emerging. As the MoO3 content increased, the intensity of the characteristic peak of MoO3 was observed to strengthen, signifying the successful MoO3 loading on g-C3N4-x.

The surface chemically bonded functional groups of catalysts were investigated by FTIR measurements, as presented in Fig. 2b. The absorption peak of 808 cm−1 in the g-C3N4-x spectra was assigned to vibration of the triazine structure. The distinct absorption band in the 1240–1650 cm−1 region was generated by the C-N and C=N bonds and the 3000–3500 cm−1 region was correspond to the N-H groups [34]. The distinct peaks in the MoO3 spectra at 868 cm−1 and 988 cm−1 coincided with the stretch varies of Mo-O-Mo bridging bond and the stretching mode of Mo = O bond, respectively [35,36]. Once MoO3 was coupled with g-C3N4-x, the weak stretching modes of Mo = O bond and the absorption peaks of C-N heterocycles coexisted in the CNM heterojunctions, illustrating that the heterojunction architecture of the CNM materials were successfully fabricated. Noteworthily, as doping with MoO3 content rose, the characteristic peak intensity of the triazine structure gradually decreased, elucidating that MoO3 exhibited strong interactions with g-C3N4-x.

The constituent and chemical condition of the catalysts were further studied by XPS. As depicted in Fig. 3 a, the full-scan XPS spectrum of the CNM-5 scanning reveals the coexistence of the elements C, N, Mo, and O, without additional impurity signals. The C1s spectra was composed of four peaks (Fig. 3b). Among these, the peaks at 284.8 eV, 286.5 eV, 288.1 eV, and 289.3 eV were assigned to the C-C bond, C-O bond, N-C=N group, and N-C-N group, respectively [37,38]. The N1s spectrum depicted in Fig. 3c, the binding energy of 398.6 eV, 399.8 eV, 400.8 eV and 404.2 eV were associated with C-N=C vibration, N-C3, −NHx and π-π* bonding, respectively [39,40]. The C1s and N1s spectra of CNM-5 exhibit a minor deviation in comparison to g-C3N4-x, which can be ascribed to insufficient doping of molybdenum oxide. In addition, the formation of N defect in g-C3N4-x can be verified by the ratio of surface atomic composition, the ratio of N2C peak area to (N3C+C-NHx) is compared in the N1s fine spectrum of g-C3N4 and g-C3N4-x, it ranges from 3.14 to 2.83 (Table S1), suggesting a decrease in the N2C structure [41,42].

The Mo 3d spectrum of CNM-5 shown in Fig. 3d, the four peaks for CNM-5, at 232.9 eV, 236.1 eV and 234.8 eV coincided with Mo 3d5/2, Mo 3d3/2 from Mo6+ and Mo 3d5/2, Mo 3d3/2 from Mo5+, respectively [43,44]. The presence of Mo5+ peaks were attribute to the oxygen vacancy of the prepared MoO3 sample. Compared to pure MoO3, the binding peak of Mo element in CNM-5 shifts towards lower binding energies. This shift was ascribed to the electronic transfer occurring between g-C3N4-x and MoO3, which suggests a strong coupling interaction at the interfaces of the two semiconductors, indirectly verifying the successful construction of the CNM heterojunction materials.

Meanwhile, nitrogen defects in the samples were further examined by EPR. As depicted in Fig. S2a, both g-C3N4 and g-C3N4-x exhibit symmetric radical signal at g = 2.0042, suggesting the presence of unpaired electrons in the conjugated aromatic ring, which was attributed to the absence of nitrogen atoms on a neighboring sp2-C. The higher paramagnetic signal observed for g-C3N4-x compared to g-C3N4 indicates a higher nitrogen defect content. Combined with the conclusion of XPS, the possible structure of g-C3N4-x was illustrated in Fig. S2b.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optoelectrical properties of CNM heterojunction photocatalyst
ce:section.ce:para:
The light absorption of the catalysts was measured by UV–visible spectrometer, as illustrated in Fig. 4 a. The absorption edge of g-C3N4-x exhibited a redshift compared to that of g-C3N4, which possibly arose from the fact that the nitrogen defect in g-C3N4-x forms a defect level, improving the ability of g-C3N4-x to capture photons, thus expanding the range of light absorption [45,46]. The CNM heterojunction exhibits an optical absorption edge ranging from 451 nm to 458 nm, suggesting a shift in the absorption characteristics of g-C3N4-x upon coupling with MoO3. As depicted in Fig. 4b, the Kubelka-Munk function can be utilized to determine the bandgap width
E g
of CNM-5, g-C3N4, g-C3N4-x, and MoO3, with calculated values of 2.75 eV, 2.70 eV, 2.62 eV, and 2.81 eV, respectively. Additionally, the spectrum of the XPS valence band (Fig. 4c) revealed the
E
VB
values of g-C3N4, g-C3N4-x, and MoO3 to be 1.64 eV, 1.74 eV, and 3.10 eV, respectively. The
E
CB
values of the catalysts were computed according to equation (2):
(2)
E
VB
=
E
CB
+
E g

The
E
CB
values of the catalysts are −1.06 eV, −0.88 eV, and 0.28 eV, respectively. Based on the computation results, the band structures of g-C3N4, g-C3N4-x, and MoO3 were depicted in Fig. 4d. The comparison of these band structures reveals that the staggered energy band alignment observed between g-C3N4-x and MoO3 suggests the potential for forming a Z-scheme heterostructure. This staggered band alignment facilitates charge transfer across the interface, which is a key feature for the Z-scheme mechanism. The specific type of heterostructure and its implications will be further explored in ESR analysis.

To appraise the photoexcited charges separation and recombination process of the catalysts, the PL, time-dependent photocurrent and EIS were performed. The PL spectra of the photocatalysts at 385 nm excitation-wavelength were displayed in Fig. 5 a. The PL peak intensity of g-C3N4-x is significantly lower than that of g-C3N4, indicating that nitrogen defects can efficiently mitigate the recombination of photogenerated carriers. After decorating the surface of g-C3N4-x with MoO3, the emission intensity of the CNM heterojunctions was observed to decrease remarkably. This result suggested that the electron holes pairs in heterojunction were less prone to recombination, and more electron-hole pairs will participate in the redox process. Notably, the CNM-5 sample showed the weakest photoluminescence intensity. In the subsequent tetracycline degradation experiment, it exhibited the highest degradation efficiency. Therefore, these results have led us to select the CNM-5 sample for further investigation.

In order to further demonstrate the excellent characteristics of CNM-5, the sample was tested by time-dependent photocurrent. The curve in Fig. 5b indicated that the photocurrent value of the CNM-5 catalyst was 0.36 μA/cm2, which was 1.44 times, 2.12 times and 2.77 times that of g-C3N4-x (0.25 μA/cm2), g-C3N4 (0.17 μA/cm2) and MoO3 (0.13 μA/cm2), indicating the outstanding efficiency of the photogenerated carrier separation and charge transfer in the heterojunctions. A lesser intrinsic charge-transportation resistance is reflected in a smaller arc radius [47]. It can be presented from the EIS curve shown in Fig. 5c, indicating that the conductivity of the sample follows the order CNM-5 > g-C3N4-x > g-C3N4.This shows that CNM-5 has the stronger electrical conductivity, these results coincided with the conclusion of PL test, indicating that the heterojunction components effectively promote carriers to separate and thus increase the photocatalytic performance.

In this experiment, the degradation efficiency of tetracycline was evaluated using visible light irradiation for all prepared catalysts. Fig. 6 a showed the visible-light (>400 nm) photodegradation ability of the CNM with various amounts of MoO3. The blank test confirmed that under visible light irradiation, MoO3 alone had only adsorption effect on tetracycline, and its photolysis effect was negligible. The adsorptive capacity of MoO3 is due to its wide interlayer spacing and strong electronegativity, which allows for the effective penetration and binding of small molecules such as tetracycline. However, the photocatalytic activity of pure MoO3 under visible light is limited by the inefficient separation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs. The photocatalytic performance of CNM was significantly enhanced following MoO3 modification. However, when the doping amount of MoO3 exceeded 5 %, the degradation ability of tetracycline decreased. This can be interpreted as suggesting that MoO3 may act as a nucleation site, covering the active site on the g-C3N4-x surface, thereby reducing the effectiveness of carrier transfer. Specifically, CNM-5 achieved a remarkable 76 % photodegradation of TC after 40 min of visible light irradiation, whereas g-C3N4-x could only remove 55.8 % of TC.

Meanwhile, the first-order pseudo kinetic equation can be used to further assess the degradation data, as exhibited in Fig. 6b. The apparent rate constant k of CNM-5 catalysts was calculated as 0.04907 min−1, which was 2.47 folds greater than the value of g-C3N4-x (k = 0.01985 min−1) during the initial 20 min of the reaction. Additionally, the impact of varying catalyst concentrations on the degradation process have been accounted, show in Fig. S4. Excessive amounts of catalyst can lead to a decrease in the light absorption rate of the semiconductor material, resulting in a reduction of catalytic capability.

To assess the cyclic stability of CNM-5, four degradation cycles were conducted. As shown in Fig. 6c, in the subsequent fourth cycle test, the photocatalytic degradation efficiency of CNM-5 experienced 20 % decline, which may be attributed to minor losses during the collection of catalysts or to photocorrosion of the catalysts during the degradation process. Following a quartet of utilization cycles, XRD and SEM analyses were performed to assess the structural and morphological steadfastness of the sample, as depicted in Fig. S5 and Fig. S6. The XRD spectra revealed an invariant alignment of the diffraction peak positions, matching those of the pristine material. Subsequently, SEM inspections confirmed the sustained topographical integrity of the photocatalyst, with no discernible signs of deterioration or aggregation, thereby indicating its stability for practical applications.

In addition, the degradation performance of other similar samples was compared, as shown in Table 1 . These reports indicated that the degradation rate in this study was the fastest, occurring in the shortest time under specific conditions of initial pollutant concentration, photocatalyst dose, and reaction time.

To further disclose the photocatalytic mechanism, the signal changes of •O2 – and •OH in g-C3N4-x and CNM-5 under dark and irradiation conditions were evaluated using ESR, which further revealed the relationship between the formation of active substances and the specific type of heterojunctions. As exhibited in Fig. 7 (a-d), neither substance has DMPO-•O2 – or DMPO-•OH signal peaks in the dark condition. After 10 min of exposure to visible light, g-C3N4-x still showed no DMPO-•OH signal, but a weak DMPO-•O2 – signal was observed. In contrast, the remarkable signals of DMPO-•O2 – and DMPO-•OH were simultaneously examined in CNM-5, proving that the photogenerated e– with excellent reduction capacity reserved on the CB of g-C3N4-x and the photogenerated h+ with remarkable oxidation capacity reserved on the VB of MoO3. Therefore, the charge-transfer route in the CNM materials does not proceed via the conventional type II heterojunction structure.

To further analyze the key active ingredients that function in the photocatalyst, the active free radicals generated during photocatalytic degradation of TC were evaluated by radical capturing test. PBQ, TEOA, and IPA were respectively introduced into the CNM-5 as quencher agents of superoxide radical (•O2 –), hole (h+), and hydroxyl radical (•OH) to investigate the photodegradation mechanism. The addition of PBQ as a quencher resulted in a considerable decrease in TC degrading efficiency, down to 26.56 % as exhibited in Fig. 7(c-d), signifying that •O2 – is crucial in the photocatalytic TC degradation process. After adding IPA, the degradation efficiency decreased to 60.15 %. The results confirmed that •OH was also a capitalactive ingredient for TC photodegradation. Interestingly, the introduction of TEOA had minimal effect on the photodegradation of TC by CNM-5, possibly due to the rapid depletion of holes, facilitating the separation of h+ and e− [54]. These findings provided valuable insights into the photocatalytic mechanism and the role of various active species in the degradation of TC.

The Mott-Schottky plot depicted in Fig. S7 has ascertained the flat band potentials (Efb) for both MoO3 and g-C3N4. The Efb of n-type semiconductor is approximately equal to the Fermi level (EF) [55,56]. The Efb of g-C3N4 is more negative than that of MoO3, indicating a spontaneous flow of electrons from g-C3N4 to MoO3, thereby establishing an internal electric field (IEF) until the Fermi levels equilibrate. On the g-C3N4 side, the depletion of electrons results in the formation of a hole accumulation layer, which causes the bands of g-C3N4 to bend upwards. Concurrently, on the MoO3 side, an electron accumulation layer forms, leading to downward band bending at the MoO3 interface. Under the influence of the IEF, the curvature of the bands, and the force of Coulombic attraction, the photoexcited electrons within the conduction band of MoO3 are compelled to migrate towards and subsequently recombine with the photogenerated holes present in the valence band of g-C3N4. Based on the previously mentioned findings, a plausible mechanism for photodegradation of TC by CNM Z-scheme heterojunction photocatalysts was described in Fig. 8 . When the composite was exposed to visible-light irradiation, both g-C3N4-x and MoO3 produce photogenerated electrons and hole. Simultaneously, the photogenerated electrons of MoO3 recombined with the holes of g-C3N4-x, which allows the electrons and holes with greater redox capacity to be preserved. These more redox electron and hole combine with O2 and H2O on the samples surface to generate •O2 – and •OH, respectively. Thus, the g-C3N4-x/MoO3 Z-scheme heterostructure can remarkably increase the capability of photogenerated carrier separation and speed up the photodegradation of tetracycline by generating •O2 – and •OH.

To verify the practical application value of photocatalysts, investigating the composition and toxicity of intermediate products in photocatalytic reactions is vital. In this paper, the intermediates TC of photodegradation by CNM-5 was identified using the Liquid Chromatography coupled to Mass Spectrometry (LC-MS) method. The content, mass spectrum ratio and structure of the different intermediates identified in solution were depicted in Table S2 and Fig. S3. According to the literature and obtained results, Fig. 9 depicts Two possible degradation pathways dominated by •O2 – and •OH, respectively [57,58]. In the first pathway, TC molecule (m/z = 445) undergone a series of reactions that results in the loss of the N-demethylation group that was caused by •OH and h+, leading to the formation of P1 (m/z = 432) [59]. P1 was oxidatively cleaved through oxidative dehydroxylation and ring-opening generating intermediates P3 (m/z = 360) and then the deacetylation group further loss to form P5 (m/z = 228). Ultimately, P5 might be oxidatively cleaved through the deamination, dihydroxylation and ring-cracking to convert the P7 (m/z = 158) and P9 (m/z = 114) [60]. In the second pathway, TC molecule (m/z = 445) undergone hydroxylation cleaved through N-demethylation, dehydroxylation and dealkylation, further transforming into to generate intermediate P2 (m/z = 400), further ring-cleavage and oxidative disintegration promoted the generation of P4 (m/z = 330), P6 (m/z = 228) and P8 (m/z = 136) [61,62]. These small molecules eventually generated carbon dioxide and water through the process of ring-opening, demethylation, dehydration and decarbonization.

In order to estimate the toxicity of TC and decomposition intermediate products, we simulated a period of 96 h lethality test for fathead minnows by using T.E.S.T methodologies and QSAR model, as shown in Table S3. The acute toxicity of tetracycline drugs was greatly decreased after photocatalytic reaction (Fig. 10 a), and the LC50 of the intermediate products was reduced to varying degrees, and the fathead minnow LC50 of the final product P9 could be confirmed as “not harmful”. In addition, the developmental toxicity of tetracycline was 0.86, which was located in the “developmental toxicity” stage (Fig. 10b). Although the intermediate products after photodegradation showed “developmental toxicity”, the final products P8 (0.36) and P9 (0.34) were “developmental non-toxic”, indicating that the photocatalytic process drastically decreased the developmental toxic of tetracycline. Finally, as illustrated in Fig. 10c, tetracycline is a “mutagenic positive” drug. Except for P1 and P3, which are still classified as “mutagenic positive”, the other products are classified as “mutagenic negative”. These results indicate that the CNM-5 Z-scheme catalysts can successfully reduce the toxicity of TC.

In summary, we have successfully fabricated a Z-scheme heterojunction of CNM materials through a straightforward blending calcination approach, which is utilized for the rapid degradation of tetracycline. From various characterization techniques, the introduction of N-defect enlarged the range of light absorption, and the formation of Z-scheme heterostructure further enhanced the efficiency of charge separation and the electron mobility. The CNM-5 catalyst showed a 76 % removal efficiency of TC within 40 min, degradation rate constant was 2.47 higher than that of g-C3N4-x. Radical capturing test exhibited that •O2 – and •OH were the primary active species for photocatalytic removal of TC. LC-MS results demonstrated that the essential photodegradation mechanism mainly involved the demethylation and oxidation on the different side chain of tetracycline. The toxicity evaluation proved that CNM can effectively alleviate the toxicity of TC solution, and its use in water pollution control is environmentally friendly. This study provides novel insights for the effective elimination of antibiotics from aqueous environments by nitrogen defect-rich and heterojunction construction collaborative modification.
